chapters.title: To fight racism we must drop stereotypes and reset our definitions
chapters.text:
You cannot fight an enemy you do not understand. Racism is an enemy of humanity, and it hits Black people hardest. To have a real chance against it, we need to know what it actually is.

Racist ideas love believers, not thinkers, as Ibram X. Kendi has put it.

The idea of race was created by a Portuguese prince named Henry the Navigator to justify the slave trade. Since then, the idea has grown into government policies, cultural beliefs, and personal habits that affect everyone, no matter their skin color.

For Kendi, the topic runs deep. During a high school speech contest, he shamed Black students for falling short of the standard set by Martin Luther King Jr. He won the contest, but he has since felt deep regret for leaning on stereotypes to judge his peers.

To push back against racism and what it stands for, we have to drop stereotypes, rethink what racism means to us, and shift our view of the world.

What does it actually mean to be racist. A racist is a person who supports a racist policy or behavior through their actions, inactions, or interactions with others.

People of color are not exempt. When you back a racist policy or stay silent about it, you are endorsing racism.

Very few people will openly say they are racist. In fact, many good people call themselves not racist. The problem is that not racist sounds like neutral, and no one is neutral on racism. The opposite of racist is not not racist. It is antiracist. We have to move from not racist to antiracist.

What is the gap between not racist and antiracist. Not racist means accepting the way things are. It says you do not see a need to attack the racial hierarchy all around us, which is a quiet kind of support for racism.

Being antiracist, on the other hand, means taking a clear stand. It means pushing for racial equity in society and in government policy. It calls for a real effort to question your own beliefs and habits that feed racism, no matter your skin color.

The idea of race was invented to defend the slave trade.

chapters.title: Fighting racism requires clear definitions of its core ideas
chapters.text:
All racist people share a habit. They keep shifting the meaning of racism to fit the moment. That makes it hard to call them out on what they say or do. The moving targets let racists clear themselves whenever they act on their bias. It is a bit like asking a criminal to explain the law. He will always come out ahead.

Racism keeps going because it is constantly redefined to clear itself.

To answer this, antiracists have to understand what racial inequality means and how to spot it.

Racial inequality happens when one racial group is seen as better or worse than another. It builds a hierarchy that touches everyone inside the groups. Any policy that creates or supports that hierarchy is a racial policy.

No policy is truly race neutral. It either feeds inequality or pushes for equality. A policy that backs racism is a racial policy. That covers written and unwritten rules, government actions, social beliefs, and regulations.

Some people try to soften the idea of racial policy by adding terms like systemic racism, structural racism, and institutional racism. These labels confuse people who are not used to academic talk and pull attention away from the real issue. Racial policy is enough to cover any rule, guideline, regulation, or culture that creates and keeps racial inequality in place.

Racism is created and held up by racial policy. To fight racism, we have to fight racial policy.

When racial policies take hold, they create racial discrimination. Discrimination is a product of policy, not a cause. To deal with racism, we have to look at the policies, beliefs, and rules that are in force in a society.

Discrimination on its own is not the problem. What matters is the impact. If discrimination builds racial equality, it is antiracist. If it builds racial inequality, it is racist. The goal is to push for the first kind.

chapters.title: Antiracists must take a clear stand and back antiracist discrimination
chapters.text:
Antiracist discrimination cannot grow in a world of not racist. It takes a clear stand and positive action against racism. We have to see that the current power structure feeds racism, and we have to push to take it apart.

In 1978, Supreme Court Justice Harry Blackmun wrote that to deal with racism, we first have to recognize that it exists and that it causes harm. He argued that reaching equality can sometimes mean treating people differently along the way.

Racial discrimination can only be answered with antiracist discrimination. Without it, the fight against racism will not get anywhere.

Racism cannot be fought from the side lines. It has to be challenged again and again, out loud. Saying you are not racist is not enough. You have to work to undo the harm that racism has done. That is where antiracist discrimination comes in.

Picture bringing someone who has been in chains to a race against trained athletes and expecting them to compete. The only way to give that person a fair shot is to train and equip them until they are ready to run with the rest.

People who flinch at the idea of racial discrimination are the same people who would expect a former prisoner to beat trained athletes on equal terms. No matter how many times the race is run, the trained athletes will keep the edge.

Refusing to back antiracist discrimination is a quiet vote for the way things are.

To level the field, every racial group has to get equal chances. That only happens if groups that have been held back receive extra support.

As an antiracist, you have to stay active in antiracist discrimination. This is not easy. It means looking closely at your basic ideas, the policies you support, and your stance on antiracist discrimination. You have to ask if you are living a racist idea or an antiracist one.

chapters.title: Our basic assumptions about racism shape whether we stay racist or go antiracist
chapters.text:
What is the deep idea that shapes your thinking on racism. Who is to blame. Why does it keep going. How can it be fixed.

Your answer to those questions shows the deeper idea you carry about how the world should work. Your answer also shows whether you lean toward assimilation, segregation, or antiracism.

An assimilationist sees racial groups as inferior and pushes enrichment programs to fix that inferiority. If you think racism happens because a racial group is weaker in behavior or culture, you are an assimilationist.

An assimilationist wants others to match a certain behavior and culture. Those who do not are seen as lesser.

A segregationist also sees racial groups as inferior, but believes that inferiority cannot be fixed. If you think some racial groups can never be equal, you are a segregationist.

A segregationist wants to keep racial groups apart, since they believe some groups can never develop like others.

Both the assimilationist and the segregationist hold racist ideas, since they both see one racial group as lesser than another. The only real difference is that the assimilationist thinks the gap is temporary, while the segregationist thinks it is permanent.

An antiracist holds that all racial groups are equal. No culture is above another. If you believe racial groups are equal, you are an antiracist.

To fight racism, you have to fight both assimilation and segregation. The belief that one racial group is lesser than another is the root of racism. No matter the motive, assimilation and segregation both lead to racial policy, not antiracist policy.

These three ideas, assimilation, segregation, and antiracism, shape racial policy and the way people see the world.

chapters.title: Biological racism rests on false claims and must be met with biological antiracism
chapters.text:
In 1988, a Black hypertension researcher made a striking claim. He said African Americans were more likely to have hypertension because their ancestors drank salt water from the Atlantic Ocean during the Middle Passage.

The claim was not tested in a lab or checked by the press. Clarence Grim, the researcher, came up with the idea while reading Roots by Alex Haley. Even so, the media treated it as fact.

Today, plenty of people still hold false ideas about biological differences between racial groups. These myths are kept alive by junk science, untested theories, readings of religious texts, and promoters of biological racism.

A biological racist is someone who argues that some racial groups are biologically different and that those differences make one group better than another.

Biological racists have tried many ways to make their ideas sound real. The Bible has been used to back the slave trade. Charles Darwin's work on evolution has been twisted into a claim that some racial groups have evolved while others have not.

These myths leave the so called weaker races with three options, assimilation, slavery, or extinction. When members of those groups accept the idea, they have to try to fix their so called biological flaws, accept their place as lesser humans, or wait for extinction.

That way of thinking narrows what people think they can achieve. Over time, it builds a system of self oppression where members of the weaker group hold themselves back with their own thoughts. They believe they can never match the so called superior group, and the belief becomes a self fulfilling prophecy.

The false idea that the biology of one race gives it special powers over another holds those races back.

The answer is to teach the biological antiracist idea that all races share the same basic biology. Surface differences like skin color do not create any meaningful gap in biology.

No racial group is lesser than another because of genetic differences. The biology of the races is essentially the same.

chapters.title: An antiracist rejects ethnic, cultural, and behavioral racism
chapters.text:
Ethnic racism shows up when you believe that people of a certain ethnic background are better than others. An example is the claim that African Americans are less intelligent and hard working than immigrants from African countries.

Ethnic racism is built on racist policies that treat some ethnic groups as better than others.

Bodily racism is the belief that some racial groups have physical traits that make them animal like and more violent. African Americans are often on the receiving end of this view. Unarmed Black Americans have been shot by police officers who saw them as a threat.

Bodily racism is the idea that some racial groups are more violent than others. It puts the lives of people in the targeted group at risk.

Cultural racism calls the culture of certain groups lesser. African American hip hop culture has often been labeled deviant and uncivilized.

Cultural racism uses the standards of one racial group to judge other cultures as inferior.

Behavioral racism leans on stereotypes. It uses the behavior of one member of a racial group to describe the whole group. It also uses the behavior of a whole group to describe a single member.

All of these forms of racism are wrong and should be met with antiracism.

For ethnic racism, all racial groups should be treated as equal, no matter their origin. The same goes for cultural racism. Cultural standards should be set aside in favor of cultural equality.

Bodily racism can be fought when we see every person as a human being with feelings. Violent and nonviolent behavior should be judged on an individual basis.

chapters.title: Colorism is a quiet form of racism that Black people can carry toward other Black people
chapters.text:
Some Black people want to be seen as Black, but not too Black. They lighten their eye color, use special creams, and try other methods to look lighter. They want straighter hair, thinner noses, and a different body shape. In short, they try to look as close to white as they can.

The reason is the racist idea that to be American is to be white, and to be white is to not be Black. That creates a clash in identity and a fog around what to take pride in. Is the pride in being Black, or in the ability to look white.

The deeper idea behind quiet racism among African Americans is colorism.

Colorism is a set of racist policies that push the idea that lighter skinned people are better than darker skinned people. It is a deep belief that people with different skin tones are not equal.

It keeps a kind of racism based on skin color alive. Members of different skin tone groups discriminate against each other to feel superior. They set rules to keep others out of their circles.

White people use the one drop rule. If you have even a drop of Black blood, you can never be fully white.

Black people use the two drop rule. If you have two drops of white blood, you can never be seen as Black.

Light skinned people use the three drop rule. If you have three drops of Black blood, you are too Black to be white.

In truth, none of these color groups are pure. That does not matter. Skin color has been used to discriminate against others.

For example, light skinned people may believe that darker skinned people are violent. They then refuse to mix with them. Light skinned people are seen as odd by the wider Black community and looked down on.

The infighting did not change the wider view of white people. To them, any drop of Black blood makes you too Black to be white.

To fight colorism, a person has to take a color antiracist stance.

Color antiracism is a set of antiracist policies that push the idea that light and dark people are equal.

There will always be pushback against color antiracism. Any antiracist policy that tries to break down colorism will be called racist by white people. Antiracists should not get distracted.

Colorism can only be fought with antiracist policy. To keep going, the antiracist has to stay self aware, keep learning, and keep checking themselves.

chapters.title: The fight against racism can be won with steady self awareness, learning, and self check
chapters.text:
Fighting racism is not something that can be done from the side lines. Most racists are locked into their views and will not give them up easily. To win, we have to see that the fight runs on two fronts, with ourselves and with the people who push racism.

The fight has to be waged on the personal front and the social front.

On the personal front, we have to keep challenging our idea of racism and the racial policies we quietly support. When we ignore the many Black babies who die each year because of poor access to health care, we quietly back a racist policy.

Sometimes we look at Black people and blame them for their struggles. We expect them to lift themselves up and take their rightful place. We forget that they have been told for generations that they do not belong. To help, we have to take a clear stand for antiracism.

Americans have long been trained to see the flaws of people rather than the flaws of policy, as Ibram X. Kendi has said.

When we become antiracist, we start to shift the policies in our society. Policies drive racial discrimination, not the other way around. Racist leaders can be voted out, and discriminatory policies can be rewritten. We have to know how to spot racist policies first.

Any policy built on a racist idea, the idea that one racial group is lesser or greater than another, is a racial policy. Any policy that creates and keeps racial discrimination in place is also a racial policy.

Antiracism calls on us to challenge racial policies.

chapters.title: A path to a more equal society
chapters.text:
If we want to be antiracist, we have to understand why racism exists and why past efforts have failed to build an antiracist society. Understanding racial failure means understanding failed solutions. When we get those failed solutions, we also get the root of racism and racial ideas. To cut racial inequity in our spaces, we have to do the following. Acknowledge that racial inequity is a problem, and blame policy, not people. Spot racial ideas in every form they take. Build antiracist policies. Teach ourselves and others about racial policy and antiracist policy. Push hard against racist policy makers to put antiracist policies in place. Watch closely to see whether the antiracist policies are actually reducing racial inequity. Stay alert for new racial policies that may show up.

Doing this can lower racial inequity and weaken racial policy, with the hope that antiracists will win the fight.

Try this

Make a list of racial policies that have shaped your spaces. Decide which antiracist policies could replace them.
